does: This paper develops a modified SISA framework for machine unlearning that enables removal of entire classes from trained CNNs without full retraining. The method partitions the neural network into shards during training, isolates class information within specific shards, and uses a gating network alongside a reinforced replay mechanism to selectively forget target classes while maintaining performance on retained classes. Experiments on multiple image datasets demonstrate that the approach achieves effective class-level unlearning with significantly reduced computational cost compared to full model retraining, while preserving accuracy on non-target classes.
connects: This work builds on the machine unlearning literature that emerged in response to data privacy regulations like GDPR, extending prior work on SISA architectures originally developed for efficient machine learning at scale. It responds to the specific challenge of class-level unlearning as distinguished from instance-level removal, and incorporates gating mechanisms and replay-based training strategies that draw from continual learning and catastrophic forgetting literature. The paper addresses practical concerns raised by the rapid deployment of large vision models trained on proprietary data, connecting to broader discussions of data rights and consent in modern machine learning.
enables: This framework could support practical implementation of user data deletion requests in production systems, reducing the computational burden of maintaining machine learning models that respect privacy constraints. The gating network approach may generalize to other selective forgetting scenarios beyond class removal, such as attribute-level or concept-level unlearning. Future work could extend the method to larger-scale models, multimodal systems, and generative architectures, potentially enabling privacy-preserving model maintenance as a standard practice rather than exceptional case. The efficiency gains could make unlearning feasible for organizations regularly handling user deletion requests.
